seoTitle: How to Add an Animated GIF to an Outlook Email
metaDescription: Insert an animated GIF into an Outlook email using the ribbon's inline image tool or a simple drag-and-drop, while keeping accessibility and file size in mind.
intro: Plain text can feel flat when you want an email to carry more energy or personality. This guide covers embedding an animated GIF inline using Outlook's ribbon or a quick drag-and-drop, resizing it properly, and adding alt text before sending a test to confirm the loop plays correctly.

steps.heading: Open a new email
steps.body: Start a new message and position your cursor inside the message body.

steps.heading: Insert the image
steps.body: On desktop, go to the Insert tab and select Pictures Inline; on the web, use the bottom toolbar's image tool to browse your device.

steps.heading: Or drag and drop
steps.body: Alternatively, drag your local .gif file directly into the draft pane.

steps.heading: Resize proportionally
steps.body: Grab a corner anchor to resize the image without stretching or distorting it.

steps.heading: Add alt text
steps.body: Add descriptive alt text so the image remains accessible to screen reader users.

steps.heading: Send a test email
steps.body: Send a test message to yourself to verify the animation loops properly for the recipient.

faq.question: Will the GIF animate for every recipient?
faq.answer: Most modern email clients including Outlook desktop, web, and mobile support animated GIFs, though very old clients may only show a static first frame.

faq.question: Why does my GIF look frozen after I send it?
faq.answer: This can happen due to local system performance settings, so check your Advanced Performance preferences if animations appear static.

faq.question: Is there a file size limit I should worry about?
faq.answer: Yes, large GIF files can trigger spam filters or bounce due to attachment size limits, so keeping the file compressed helps delivery.

faq.question: Should I optimize the first frame of my GIF?
faq.answer: Yes, setting a strong first frame is a good practice in case a recipient's client only displays a static preview.
